bibliographic_data.title: Dynamic Functional Assessment of Hand Motion Using an Animation Glove: The Effect of Stenosing Tenosynovitis
bibliographic_data.abstract: Background: The aim of the present study is to determine whether an animation glove can be utilized to provide a reliable and reproducible assessment of dynamic hand function and whether this assessment is altered in the setting of hand pathology. Methods: Ten subjects without known hand pathology and 11 subjects with known stenosing tenosynovitis were assessed on tasks involving hand function at varied speeds, including forceful and gradual making of a fist and the quick and slow grip of a baseball using an animation glove to record range of motion and measures of velocity (CyberGlove II). Results: In normal subjects, peak extension and flexion velocity of the index and middle finger was highest in the metacarpophalangeal and lowest in the distal interphalangeal; however, the converse was true in the ring finger. In those subjects with stenosing tenosynovitis, the animation glove was able to detect a triggering event during assessment. Furthermore, there was a significant decrease in the maximum velocity of the proximal interphalangeal joint observed with the slow fist task in both flexion and extension (55%, P < .01) in the affected hand when compared with the unaffected hand. Conclusions: The CyberGlove II can be utilized in the dynamic functional analysis of the hand and is able to detect a triggering event in subjects with known stenosing tenosynovitis. Those subjects demonstrate a significant decrease in maximum velocity in slow fist tasks, highlighting the need for comprehensive assessment to ascertain the full extent of functional limitations that can occur in the setting of hand pathology.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study demonstrates the use of an animation glove (CyberGlove II) to assess dynamic hand function and detect triggering events in stenosing tenosynovitis, providing a more accurate assessment than static methods.
core_content.key_methodology: Ten healthy subjects and 11 patients with known stenosing tenosynovitis were assessed using the CyberGlove II for tasks involving varied speeds of making a fist and gripping a baseball. The glove recorded range of motion and measures of velocity.
outcomes: In normal subjects, peak extension and flexion velocities varied by finger joint type.
The animation glove detected triggering events in patients with stenosing tenosynovitis.
Patients with stenosing tenosynovitis showed a significant decrease in maximum proximal interphalangeal joint velocity during slow fist tasks.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
Introduction: Functional assessment of hand motion is crucial for evaluating pre- and postsurgical function, as well as work-related evaluations. Most assessments focus on static measurements using goniometry and grip/pinch strength devices, which may not accurately reflect the dynamic nature of hand function due to muscle substitution and inventiveness observed in patients with pathological conditions. The study aimed to develop a battery of tasks using the CyberGlove II for real-time assessment of hand motion.

Methods: Ten healthy subjects without known hand pathology and 11 patients with stenosing tenosynovitis were assessed on tasks involving varied speeds, including making a fist forcefully or gradually and gripping a baseball quickly or slowly. The CyberGlove II recorded range of motion and measures of velocity to assess dynamic hand function.

Results: In healthy subjects, peak extension and flexion velocities differed by finger joint type. Patients with stenosing tenosynovitis showed a significant decrease in maximum proximal interphalangeal joint velocity during slow fist tasks compared to the unaffected hand. The animation glove detected triggering events in these patients.

Conclusions: The CyberGlove II can provide reliable and reproducible assessments of dynamic hand function, particularly useful for detecting functional limitations in conditions like stenosing tenosynovitis.
